shortDescription.text: Passwords stored in plaintext without hashing

fullDescription.text: User passwords are stored as plaintext in MongoDB. The bcrypt hashing code is commented out, meaning passwords are stored exactly as received from the user. An attacker who gains read access to the database (via NoSQL injection, backup leak, or compromised admin) obtains all credentials immediately.

shortDescription.text: NoSQL injection via unsanitized query parameters

fullDescription.text: User-supplied values are passed directly into MongoDB query objects without type validation or sanitization. An attacker can supply an object like {"$gt": ""} instead of a string for userName, bypassing authentication or extracting data.

shortDescription.text: PII stored in plaintext without encryption

fullDescription.text: Sensitive personal data (SSN, date of birth, bank account, bank routing number) is stored in MongoDB in plaintext. The encryption code using crypto module is commented out. A database compromise exposes all PII directly.

shortDescription.text: Hardcoded plaintext credentials in database seed script

fullDescription.text: The database reset script contains hardcoded plaintext passwords for admin and user accounts. These credentials are committed to source control and are trivially discoverable.

shortDescription.text: Plaintext password comparison enables credential theft

fullDescription.text: The validateLogin function compares passwords using direct string equality (fromDB === fromUser) instead of bcrypt.compareSync. Combined with plaintext storage, this means the entire authentication chain provides zero cryptographic protection for credentials.

shortDescription.text: Insecure Direct Object Reference in profile update

fullDescription.text: The updateUser and getByUserId functions in profile-dao.js accept a userId parameter that is used directly in the MongoDB query. If the calling route does not enforce that the authenticated user can only access their own profile, an attacker can modify or read any user's profile by changing the userId parameter.

The codebase imports bcrypt-nodejs which is deprecated and unmaintained. Even if the commented-out hashing code were enabled, it relies on a library with known issues. The maintained alternative is the 'bcrypt' or 'bcryptjs' package.

message.text: Password is stored as plaintext. The bcrypt hashing on line 29 is commented out, so the raw password from the request is written directly to MongoDB. Any database compromise yields all user credentials in cleartext.

The userName parameter is passed directly into MongoDB findOne query without type checking. If the HTTP layer passes a parsed JSON object (e.g. {"$gt": ""}), the attacker can bypass authentication entirely by matching any user document.
The userName parameter in getUserByUserName is passed unsanitized into MongoDB findOne. An attacker-controlled object can manipulate the query operator to extract or match unintended documents.

SSN, date of birth, bank account number, and bank routing number are stored in plaintext in MongoDB. The encryption code (lines 15-40) is entirely commented out. A database breach exposes all PII without any cryptographic barrier.

Plaintext passwords ('Admin_123', 'User1_123', 'User2_123') are hardcoded in the database seed script and committed to version control. The bcrypt hashed alternatives are commented out. These default credentials are trivially discoverable and may persist in production.

Password validation uses direct string equality (fromDB === fromUser) instead of bcrypt comparison. The secure bcrypt.compareSync call on line 65 is commented out. This, combined with plaintext password storage, means the authentication system offers no cryptographic protection.

updateUser accepts a userId parameter and uses it directly in the MongoDB update query with no authorization check at the DAO layer. If the route handler does not verify that the authenticated session user matches the target userId, any authenticated user can modify another user's profile including SSN, bank account, and routing number.

The file imports bcrypt-nodejs which has been deprecated since 2017 and is no longer maintained. Even if the commented-out hashing code were re-enabled, it would rely on an unmaintained library with potential security issues. Replace with 'bcrypt' or 'bcryptjs'.
